issue_title: OpenSSL issue and possible use of urandom
issue_body:
Hi @James1345 and other contributors, first thanks for the great app!

I use Knox in production on several projects. This time I need to deploy one of those in a Linux shared server in a hosting provider (WebFaction). The problem is when I login I always receive the same token no matter the user credentials I send. Naturally, when trying to login a second time I get:

```
"(1062, "Duplicate entry '1080f871e39cc839e5bcc9f852f9a8f3ddc03cf7e72e9fd1d6e4a71d7e74936f' for key 'PRIMARY'")".
```

I noticed the problem is with the random bytes generation. When Knox tries to generate salt and token it only gets a bunch of zeros from `OpenSSL.rand.bytes`. So when it uses the zeros to digest the token, the result is always the same. This generates a database error since token field is, of course, unique.

As I needed to deploy the project fast I started to mess around with Knox code and found that if I replace the `OpenSSL.rand.bytes` with `os.urandom` it solves the problem. Is also good to mention both `os.urandom` and `OpenSSL.rand.bytes` work fine in the Python terminal of the same server.

In the documentation of Knox is said that "DRF Tokens are generated with os.urandom, which is not cryptographically secure. Knox uses OpenSSL to provide tokens." But the documentation of [pyOpenSSL rand](http://www.pyopenssl.org/en/stable/api/rand.html) module says "Functions from this module shouldn’t be used. Use urandom instead." pointing the [this](https://sockpuppet.org/blog/2014/02/25/safely-generate-random-numbers/) article.

So could some one clarify if is it really worse to use `os.random` and why?

When I run this code:
```python
from google import google
results = google.search_images("banana")
```
An `IndexError: list index out of range` occurred. 

This bug is caused by `images.py`, line `298-309`, function `_get_file_name(link)`
```python
def _get_file_name(link):

    temp_name = link.rsplit('/', 1)[1]
    image_format = _parse_image_format(link)

    if image_format and temp_name.rsplit(".", 1)[1] != image_format:
        file_name = temp_name.rsplit(".", 1)[0] + "." + image_format

    else:
        file_name = temp_name

    return file_name
```

For the "banana" image search, the problem is cause by this link: `http://vignette2.wikia.nocookie.net/donkeykong/images/1/17/BananaDKCR.png/revision/20110926232526` 

Given 
```python
link = "http://vignette2.wikia.nocookie.net/donkeykong/images/1/17/BananaDKCR.png/revision/20110926232526"
```
in `_get_file_name(link)`, we have 
```python
temp_name = "20110926232526"
image_format = "png"
```
Since `temp_name` has no `.`, `temp_name.rsplit(".", 1)` is a list of length 1 and  `temp_name.rsplit(".", 1)[1]` doesn't exist.

I think it's better to change `temp_name.rsplit(".", 1)[1]` to `temp_name.rsplit(".", 1)[-1]`, which will solve this problem.

I just want to deactivate the color-escape sequences but not the other settings (e.g. format).

issue_title: Enable bcrypt as default encryption setting
issue_body:
Flask-security currently stores passwords in plaintext by default. My fear is that others like me did not notice this behavior, as the only reference to it is a note in the configuration section saying 'Defaults to plaintext'. 

From researching it sounds like bcrypt is the best available option. I recommend either bundling bcrypt in the install and enabling as the default encryption setting, or adding an app.config['SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH'] to the QuickStart page in the documentation.

Django also disallows entirely numeric passwords: https://github.com/django/django/blob/09f2cdbe1a43e79e31f5ea509b59d4c87db29832/django/contrib/auth/password_validation.py#L180

This seems like a great idea too. Is this something you would mind me adding to flask-security?

issue_title: How to change response content type in swagger UI?
issue_body:
I have a [GET] route, which I wish to return a response with content-type="application/pdf". But looks like in swagger UI generated from flask-restplus we only have one response content type in json. Is there a way to change that and allow us to test that endpoint in swagger?

![screenshot from 2018-06-21 11-41-28](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/39310030/41732971-292427fc-7548-11e8-86f0-548a158423aa.png)

I'm having trouble getting JSON body inputs documented in swagger and working the way I want.

My end goal is to setup a `POST` request that is JSON that looks like `{"type": "foobar"}`.

I want to be able to validate the input coming in so I first tried doing this using the following parser:

```
parser = api.parser()

packages_post = parser.copy()
packages_post.add_argument(
        "data",
        required=True,
        location="json",
        help="The type of package to create."
)
```

The problem with this method is it doesn't really document what the schema looks like very well:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3237256/9373067/ec663dac-46b3-11e5-9157-b031edb0a2f4.png)

I then went with a model and used the `body` option in `api.doc()` which did document things well but doesn't do validation:

```
package = api.model(
        "Package",
        {
                "type": fields.String(
                        description="The type of package to create.",
                        required=True
                )
        }
)
```

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3237256/9373084/2ae2410c-46b4-11e5-87a3-019ebafeb82e.png)

Alright, so I figure I can use a combination of the two to get both the right documentation and validation:

```
package = api.model(
        "Package",
        {
                "type": fields.String(
                        description="The type of package to create.",
                        required=True
                )
        }
)

parser = api.parser()

packages_post = parser.copy()
packages_post.add_argument(
        "package",
        type=package,
        location="json"
)
```

I noticed this pattern in the tests and it almost works however now the input needs to be `{"package": {"type": "foobar"}}` which doesn't get documented very well.

The only way I can see to get things to work the way I want is to define both a parser and a model with the same exact information which is a bit ugly and redundant.

Is there something I might be missing to make this a bit better? Perhaps something I can look at adding to make this possible?
Please, make a new release if you are not planning to add more fixes soon.

The jti claim seems to be required when it should be optional.  This makes it tough to use with a provider that thinks it's optional.  Is there a way to disable this check?

```
# Make sure that any custom claims we expect in the token are present
if 'jti' not in data:
    raise JWTDecodeError("Missing claim: jti")
```

[4.1.7.  "jti" (JWT ID) Claim](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.7)

   The "jti" (JWT ID) claim provides a unique identifier for the JWT.
   The identifier value MUST be assigned in a manner that ensures that
   there is a negligible probability that the same value will be
   accidentally assigned to a different data object; if the application
   uses multiple issuers, collisions MUST be prevented among values
   produced by different issuers as well.  The "jti" claim can be used
   to prevent the JWT from being replayed.  The "jti" value is a case-
   sensitive string.  Use of this claim is OPTIONAL.

issue_title: #Not working on production deployed on aws. if debug is true works on localhost  else fails on localhost as well.

issue_body:
There is currently an [`exclude_namespaces`](http://django-rest-swagger.readthedocs.org/en/latest/settings.html#exclude-namespaces) setting, however I'd like to have an explicitly `include_namespaces` setting. 

## Proposal
A `include_namespaces` so that the swagger-ui would only display endpoints that matched the specified namespaces. This could override the existing `exclude_namespaces` in the event that an endpoint matched both `include_` and `exclude_`.

## Reasoning
I have a current site that does not have good API namespacing, but has over 30 private endpoints that I would have to specify in the current `exclude_namespaces` setting. This is a larger project with multiple developers, and I'd like to use this to start documenting publicly accessible endpoints (under a single namespace) without every dev having to remember to explicitly add new internal endpoints to the exclusion list.

## Implementation
I would be happy to implement this, but I wanted to get some feedback before hacking out the code.

issue_title: Not all module statically reachable from package root
issue_body:
By that I mean, if you run certain static analyzers on `boxsdk`, they might tell you that some modules are never used.

For example, `import boxsdk` will run `from .object import *`, and `boxsdk.object` has `folder` in its `__all__`. So at runtime, `import boxsdk` should import `boxsdk.object.folder` and include it in `sys.modules`.

However, some static analyzers aren't smart enough to realize that `folder` is imported this way, and may assume that `folder` is not used somewhere. We've found that `py2app` and `pyinstaller` both make this assumption, and will optimize `boxsdk.object.folder` out of the build, along with some other modules.

We should:
- Make sure that all modules are included at least once in a very explicit way.
- Write a test to assert this.

I've started working on this.

issue_title: Expose api to get the service `title` from the client
issue_body: Swagger spec has a `info.title` attribute which gives the service name. This can be used to label the client call by title. There should be a way in bravado and swagger-py to get it.

issue_title: documentation does not indicate how to pass global headers
issue_body:
I would like Accept: application/json to be passed with every model call. Adding a produces key to the swagger.json does not seem to do the trick. Adding request_headers to SwaggerClient.from_url only seem to work for the call to retrieve the JSON.

What's the best way to pass global headers?

issue_title: Use of option_list in Commands triggers deprecation warning in Django 1.9
issue_body:
Running `runserver_plus.py` and most other django-extensions commands produces the following deprecation warning:
```
11:26:59 web.1  | WARNING  [py.warnings:265] {snip}/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/base.py:265:
RemovedInDjango110Warning: OptionParser usage for Django management commands is deprecated, use ArgumentParser instead
```

[The docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/howto/custom-management-commands/#django.core.management.BaseCommand.option_list) advise using `add_arguments` in its place:
> You should now override the add_arguments() method to add custom arguments accepted by your
> command. See the example above.

This will be breaking in Django 1.10.

               u'behaviors': [{u'id': u'6023739659183', u'name': u'Baseball 2015'}],
               u'genders': [1],
               u'geo_locations': {u'countries': [u'US'], u'location_types': [u'home']},
               u'page_types': [u'desktopfeed', u'mobilefeed', u'rightcolumn']}}

```

You can't change the `age_max` under `targeting` by doing `a['targeting']['age_max'] = 45`. You have to do:
```python
import copy

targeting_dict = copy.deepcopy(a.remote_read(fields=[AdSet.Field.targeting]))['targeting']
targeting_dict['age_max'] = 45
a['targeting'] = targeting_dict
a.remote_update()
```

That is because line 328 in `objects.py` has the logic `if self._data[key] != value` in:
```python
    def __setitem__(self, key, value):
        """Sets an item in this CRUD object while maintaining a changelog."""

        if key not in self._data or self._data[key] != value:
            self._changes[key] = value
```

2 questions/requests:
1. Can we add an example (or documentation) that shows that we need to `copy.deepcopy()` if we're changing a value of the AdSet object that is itself a dictionary? OR
2. Can/should we change the logic of `__setitem__` to be:
```python
    def __setitem__(self, key, value):
        """Sets an item in this CRUD object while maintaining a changelog."""

        self._changes[key] = value
```

issue_title: Terminating multiprocessing.Process's with pyzmq in the mix
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm currently using pyzmq to perform some basic distributed computing. To that end, I'm running code that looks something like this:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import sys
import time
import enum
import collections
import socket
import multiprocessing
import zmq

HOSTNAME = socket.gethostname()
WORK = range(10)
TOTAL = len(WORK)


def producer():
    print("Running as producer...")
    context = zmq.Context()
    socket = context.socket(zmq.REP)
    socket.bind("tcp://*:{}".format(5555))
    for idx, i in enumerate(WORK):
        # Wait for a consumer to ask for work.
        socket.recv_pyobj()
        socket.send_pyobj(i)
        sys.stdout.write("\r{} of {} jobs distributed".format(idx + 1, TOTAL))
        sys.stdout.flush()

def consumer():
    def inner():
        print("Running as consumer for `{}`...".format(HOSTNAME))
        context = zmq.Context()
        # Create a socket to receive work items.
        socket_task = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
        socket_task.connect(
            "tcp://{host}:{port}".format(host=HOSTNAME, port=5555))
        # Create a socket to push results downstream.
        socket_result = context.socket(zmq.PUSH)
        socket_result.connect(
            "tcp://{host}:{port}".format(host=HOSTNAME, port=5556))

        # Subscribe to the command socket.
        socket_command = context.socket(zmq.SUB)
        socket_command.connect(
            "tcp://{host}:{port}".format(host=HOSTNAME, port=5557))
        socket_command.setsockopt_string(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, "")

        # Set up a poller to receive tasks/commands.
        poller = zmq.Poller()
        poller.register(socket_task, zmq.POLLIN)
        poller.register(socket_command, zmq.POLLIN)

        while True:
            # Request work.
            socket_task.send_pyobj(None)
            # Poll for requests.
            sockets = dict(poller.poll())
            # Handle a task.
            if sockets.get(socket_task) == zmq.POLLIN:
                # Retrieve work and run the computation.
                params = socket_task.recv_pyobj()
                time.sleep(1)
                result = params
                # Send the result downstream.
                socket_result.send_pyobj(result)
            # Handle a command.
            if sockets.get(socket_command) == zmq.POLLIN:
                cmd = socket_command.recv_pyobj()
                if cmd == "stop":
                    print("Stop command received")
                    # FIXME: Child processes don't seem to terminate here.
                    break

    def Process():
        proc = multiprocessing.Process(target=inner)
        proc.daemon = True
        proc.start()
        return proc
    pool = [Process() for i in range(8)]
    # Zombies are bad for your health.
    for proc in pool:
        proc.join()

def collector():
    print("Running as collector...")

    context = zmq.Context()
    socket_result = context.socket(zmq.PULL)
    socket_result.bind("tcp://*:{}".format(5556))

    socket_command = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
    socket_command.bind("tcp://*:{}".format(5557))

    n_results = 0
    while True:
        result = socket_result.recv_pyobj()
        n_results += 1
        sys.stdout.write(
            "\r{} of {} results collected".format(n_results, TOTAL))
        sys.stdout.flush()
        if n_results == TOTAL:
            break

    # Broadcast a stop instruction to shut down worker processes.
    socket_command.send_pyobj("stop")

def main():
    role = sys.argv[1]
    if role == "producer":
        producer()
    elif role == "collector":
        collector()
    elif role == "consumer":
        consumer()
    else:
        raise SystemExit("Invalid role")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

If I run this on my local machine using
```
$ python sim.py producer
$ python sim.py collector
$ python sim.py consumer
```
(in three different terminals of course), everything works fine for the most part. Each process is receiving its work items, and each process successfully transmits its results to the collector. The problem occurs when shutting down the worker processes, which never get joined for some reason. Interrupting the consumer process shows that it is hanging at `pid, sts = os.waitpid(self.pid, flag)` in `multiprocessing/popen_fork.py`. The issue seems to be that the producer and collector terminate before the consumer(s) get a chance to. If I add a simple timeout at the end of the producer and consumer functions, every process terminates properly.

Could somebody explain to me why that is, and possibly suggest a more reliable solution than adding a random timeout?

issue_title: Encryption speed slow in default install
issue_body:
I was dissatisfied with the speed of zeromq curve encryption, until I noticed that it appears to vary by an order of magnitude across different ways of installing pyzmq. This script:

```
from __future__ import print_function

import sys
import os
import time
import zmq
import zmq.auth.thread

context = zmq.Context()
auth = zmq.auth.thread.ThreadAuthenticator(context)
auth.start()
auth.configure_curve(domain='*', location=zmq.auth.CURVE_ALLOW_ANY)
del auth # Ensure auth thread doesn't keep the interpreter open

server_publickey, server_secretkey = zmq.curve_keypair()
client_publickey, client_secretkey = zmq.curve_keypair()


server = context.socket(zmq.PULL)
server.curve_publickey = server_publickey
server.curve_secretkey = server_secretkey
server.curve_server = True
port = server.bind_to_random_port('tcp://127.0.0.1')

client = context.socket(zmq.PUSH)
client.curve_secretkey = client_secretkey
client.curve_publickey = client_publickey
client.curve_serverkey = server_publickey
client.connect('tcp://127.0.0.1:%d' % port)

print('Python Version: %s' % ' | '.join(sys.version.splitlines()))
print('Platform: %s' % sys.platform)
print('pyzmq version: %s'%zmq.__version__)
print('zmq version: %s'%zmq.zmq_version())

plaintext = os.urandom(100*1024**2)
start_time = time.time()
client.send(plaintext)
assert server.recv() == plaintext
print("time to encrypt and send 100MB:", time.time() - start_time, 's')
```

Returns the following for all the different Pythons I have installed, where I have anaconda2 and anaconda3, which came with pyzmq, system python 2 and 3, for which I installed pyzmq via apt, and two virtualenvs that I made using system Python 2 and 3, and installed pyzmq using `pip  install pyzmq --install-option="--zmq=bundled"`

```
$ /usr/bin/python2.7 test.py 
Python Version: 2.7.14 (default, Sep 23 2017, 22:06:14)  | [GCC 7.2.0]
Platform: linux2
pyzmq version: 16.0.2
zmq version: 4.2.1
time to encrypt and send 100MB: 0.500539064407 s

$ /usr/bin/python3.6 test.py 
Python Version: 3.6.3 (default, Oct  3 2017, 21:45:48)  | [GCC 7.2.0]
Platform: linux
pyzmq version: 16.0.2
zmq version: 4.2.1
time to encrypt and send 100MB: 0.49707913398742676 s

$ ~/anaconda2/bin/python test.py 
Python Version: 2.7.13 |Anaconda, Inc.| (default, Sep 30 2017, 18:12:43)  | [GCC 7.2.0]
Platform: linux2
pyzmq version: 17.0.0
zmq version: 4.1.6
~/anaconda3/bin/python test.py 
time to encrypt and send 100MB: 6.23277807236 s

$ ~/anaconda3/bin/python test.py 
Python Version: 3.6.3 |Anaconda custom (64-bit)| (default, Nov  3 2017, 19:19:16)  | [GCC 7.2.0]
Platform: linux
pyzmq version: 16.0.3
zmq version: 4.2.2
time to encrypt and send 100MB: 2.81573748588562 s

$ ~/tmp/venvs/zmqtestpy2/bin/python test.py 
Python Version: 2.7.14 (default, Sep 23 2017, 22:06:14)  | [GCC 7.2.0]
Platform: linux2
pyzmq version: 17.0.0
zmq version: 4.1.6
time to encrypt and send 100MB: 6.02314901352 s

$ ~/tmp/venvs/zmqtestpy3/bin/python test.py

```

As you can see, the speed of encryption varies a lot, and in particular, is slowest with the fresh virtualenvs with the pip install and bundled zmq, and is fastest with the system versions.

At first it seems like zeromq 4.2 is what makes it fast - until you see that anaconda3 with zmq 4.2.2 is still way slower than the system ones. So it seems it's probably something about configuration rather than the specific versions of zmq in use.

So it appears maybe the bundled zeromq is a a bit slow with the encryption, and it would be better if the default install resulted in fast crypto. My users are mostly using anaconda, so if whatever ends up in anaconda could be as fast as the versions in the Ubuntu repos, that would be great!

issue_body:
I have this function that is called in a write transaction:
```
from uuid import uuid4

:

               "MERGE (topic:Topic {name: $topic}) "

                session.write_transaction(add_technique, s['title'], s['topic'])
:
```
Please note that the third parameter *uuid* of the cypher statement is passed an incorrect type because uuid4() returns a uuid.UUID instance, not a string as I thought.

When the paramater value is corrected to str(uuid4()) everything is fine.

issue_title: Feature request: combine callback calls after holding notifcations
issue_body:
In the following example:

```
from traitlets import HasTraits, Integer

class State(HasTraits):
    a = Integer()
    b = Integer()

def callback(*args, **kwargs):
    print(args, kwargs)

s = State()
s.observe(callback)
with s.hold_trait_notifications():
    s.a = 1
    s.b = 2
```

Callback gets called twice. However, it would be nice to have a way to minimize the number of callback calls when exiting the context manager.

The reason I ask about this is because I would like to consider transitioning [glue](http://www.glueviz.org) from using [echo](https://github.com/glue-viz/echo), which is our own trait-like library by traitlets. The case I describe above is common in our code, where we change a bunch of traits and then want to e.g. force a redraw (but only one and not one per trait).

issue_title: get_bucket_encryption in boto3 is not working
issue_body:
When I try to invoke get_bucket_encryption method from s3 client I see this error
'S3' object has no attribute 'get_bucket_encryption': AttributeError
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/var/task/lambda_function.py", line 54, in lambda_handler

  File "/var/runtime/botocore/client.py", line 553, in __getattr__
    self.__class__.__name__, item)
AttributeError: 'S3' object has no attribute 'get_bucket_encryption'

I've just started using boto3 (after years with the previous version) so I've been getting to grips with the new api today.

One thing that stuck out at me is how one needs to catch and inspect ClientError to determine existence when working at the resource level (eg https://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/guide/migrations3.html#accessing-a-bucket).

Maybe I'm missing something but it feels like those various exceptions should be wrapped as more specific exceptions so they could at least be caught directly. Something along the lines of:

````
try:
    s3.meta.client.head_bucket(Bucket='mybucket')
except BucketNotFoundError as e:
    pass
````

As it stands it seems I need to do a bunch of 404 checks for testing if s3 keys exist etc. Maybe there's a better way of doing it?

It does feel a little like the level of abstraction is a bit close to the underlying aws implementation - but maybe that's the intention?

As ever, thanks for an amazing and solid bit of software.

I am attempting an upload of files to S3 using `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` in AWS Lambda. This is a sample of my code:

```python
from concurrent import futures


def my_lambda(event, context):
    def upload_to_s3(file, key):
        s3.Bucket(MY_BUCKET).upload_file(file, key)

    with futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1000) as executor:
        todo = []
        for f in files:
            future = executor.submit(upload_to_s3, f, key)
            todo.append(future)

        results = []
        for future in futures.as_completed(todo):
            res = future.result()
            results.append(res)
```

It seems all the files do get upload to S3, but there doesn't seem to be any time improvement over a sequential upload. I am also getting these logs in CloudWatch:

```
Connection pool is full, discarding connection: s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com
```

Did some research and according to [this article](http://ls.pwd.io/2013/06/parallel-s3-uploads-using-boto-and-threads-in-python/) it is not possible to reuse the connection to S3 since boto3 isn't thread-safe.

Would like to know if this is indeed correct and if there is a solution to this?

Possibly related: #1128

Making a feature request and am interested in a timeline for when boto3 will incorporate the RDS ModifySnapshotAttribute call.

Our use case is automating RDS snapshots and using ModifySnapshotAttribute to share the snapshot with a separate account.

When will this method be incorporated into boto3?

issue_title: How do I create an S3 object with metadata using boto3?
issue_body:
In the example below I want to set a timestamp metadata attribute when created an S3 object. How do I do that? The documentation is not clear.

import uuuid
import json
import boto3
import botocore
import time

from boto3.session import Session
session = Session(aws_access_key_id='XXX',
                  aws_secret_access_key='XXX')

s3 = session.resource('s3')

bucket = s3.Bucket('blah')

for filename in glob.glob('json/*.json'):
    with open(filename, 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read().decode('utf-8')
        timestamp = str(round(time.time(),10))
        my_s3obj = s3.Object('blah', str(uuid.uuid4())).put(Body=json.dumps(data))

issue_title: Update documentation link in services-2.json for CloudFormation
issue_body:
Per this contribution guideline:

> We may choose not to accept pull requests that change the JSON service descriptions, such as botocore/data/aws/s3/2006-03-01/service-2.json. We generate these files upstream based on our internal knowledge of the AWS services. If there is something incorrect with or missing from a service description, it may be more appropriate to submit an issue so we can get the issue fixed upstream. This constraint only applies to the */service-2.json files.

The link http://docs.aws.amazon.com/documentation/ was introduced in commit f35e49d203889f90fead6f5ad5c0e653b54f4634. This link doesn't point to the right place for CloudFormation docs.

Here's the exact line:
https://github.com/boto/botocore/commit/f35e49d203889f90fead6f5ad5c0e653b54f4634#diff-c9eb8f09352c1c8a08ff7298a8462a03R1486

I am using boto3/botocore in AWS Lambda to perform some management tasks against the AWS API. I was noticing that some of my lambda functions would timeout when I started hammering the API and was getting throttled. It appeared that Boto3 was using it's exponential backoff logic as intended. What I am trying to figure out is if there is any kind of timeout logic to the exponential backoff logic. It appears that there is a maxRetries option available, but other than that is Boto3/botocore's default behavior to keep retrying the API with exponential backoff until AWS returns a 503/RequestLimitExceeded error or succeeds?

I am trying to figure out a good balance for my Lambda timeout time between letting Boto retry and not having my lambda run for 5 minutes.

### Issue description
Not all events are returned with API call to `/events/search` with filtering by user: `user.id=6863231661`.

The API returns only 2 events (both live, IDs: 17102491011, 17288143302), while we have 12 live events and 8 drafts.

### Software details

issue_title: Handle gmail_quote div in emails created by clicking the Reply button in gmail
issue_body:
If you click "reply" in gmail, and then change the subject line and undo the quotation in the WYSIWYG editor, you get something that looks a lot like a fresh email that you just typed yourself. If you send the email to another gmail user, it will render for them the way you would expect if you composed the email yourself from scratch.

But gmail wraps the content in a `<div class="gmail_quote">`, which gets stripped by `quotations.extract_from_html`. Ideally, talon would know when this was the case and not treat the div as a quote. This seems hard, though. It's not clear to me how to identify this case.

issue_title: fix pdb, forward stdin to the worker process on windows
issue_body:
There are 2 (probably related) errors right now.

1) When the worker process starts "things are fine" (things work if you don't need stdin). However when it restarts there is a IOError Bad File Descriptor emitted in the worker process.

2) When it starts the first time and "things are fine", if you set a pdb breakpoint in your code things will go haywire when you hit it. The process hangs and a windows dialog pops up and you can't actually use the stdin pipe to talk to pdb.

issue_title: Add methods to obtain EllipticCurvePublicNumbers from a key
issue_body:
Currently, CoseKey (and its subclasses) abstract the entire chain from parsing the public key data to using the public key to verify the signature of an arbitrary message. I can see why this is desirable, as it entirely abstracts RSA versus elliptic curve approaches.

However, it would be nice if the ES256 subclass also supported getting the actual EllipticCurvePublicNumbers object. This would allow me to use methods from the Python Cryptography library to implement an SSH agent that exports FIDO keys (well, more cleanly than my current proof-of-concept work, anyway). Some background: an SSH agent needs to report available ECDSA keys using the SEC 1 v2.0 section 2.3.3 format, which is available via the EllipticCurvePublicNumbers.encode_point() method.

It would also be nice to be able to obtain the RSAPublicNumbers objects as well (for similar reasons).

When a signature check fails, `cryptography.exceptions.InvalidSignature` is raised. How are users supposed to know to catch an exception from the underlying cryptography library?

Given that this is the reference implementation of U2F for Python servers and this is security sensitive, exceptions and error conditions should be handled better and clearer. The examples should demonstrate proper usage of the API where unhandled exceptions and crashes can appear in pretty much every line depending on user input.

My proposal: u2flib should define its own exceptions "`U2FError`" and could have different subclasses to classify input formatting errors "`U2FMalformedInput`" from signature errors "`U2FSignatureError`". Any exceptions that is raised from invoking u2flib that is not a "`U2FError`" should be treated as a bug.

issue_title: Iterating a collection using list() is very slow
issue_body:
Hello,
I'm using comtypes to automate Microsoft Word and I've run into a strange performance issue. The following code takes 14 seconds to execute on my machine:

```python
cells_in_table = [
            list(row.Cells) for row in table.Rows
            ]
```

However this code takes only 4:

```python
cells_in_table = []
for row in _rows:
    cells_in_table.append([row.Cells(i+1) for i in range(len(row.Cells))])
```

As far as I can tell using `list` to enumerate a collection is a lot slower than manually iterating it. For example the following code takes 6.1 seconds:

`list(_rows[0].Cells)`

But this code takes 1.1 seconds:

`[_rows[0].Cells(idx+1) for idx in range(len(_rows[0].Cells))]`

What could be causing the discrepancy? Working with COM is ugly enough and I like being able to use pythonic constructs, having to abandon `list()` due to performance reasons is not great. Surely the `__iter__` implementation for COM objects could just be `self(x+1) for x in range(len(self))` for most containers?

For example:

``` python
print(applies("64", "win-64"))
```

Gives `False`, not `True`.

issue_title: Support lazy wrapping of win32 functions
issue_body: While the current library has just a few functions wrapped when we start wrapping more and more of the win32 api importing the different core modules will become very expensive because of the import time wrapping. We need to revisit the implementation and support lazy wrapping of the functions.

This is so that any objects that support natural keys can also use these values as primary keys in the API when creating/updating related fields. For example:

issue_title: API to reset all; API to wrap all
issue_body:
The `init_types_collection()` function installs the tracing callbacks and sets the filename filter, but doesn't reset the globals containing collected data. Maybe it should also do that. Or maybe we need a separate API to do that. (Currently the pyannotate unit tests reset a whole bunch of globals at the start of each test -- this should become a single call.)

Second, `with collect_types.collect()` only calls `resume()` and `pause()` -- we could use another context manager that calls `init_types_collection()` and `dump_stats()` as well.

(Note that the non-context-manager APIs are also important, e.g. for use in test fixtures, where there are traditionally separate `setUp()` and `tearDown()` methods.)

issue_title: Grappelli autocomplete field in nested inline got initialized twice if parent inline has autocomplete field with same name
issue_body:
As described, consider the case below:

```
class ChildInline(NestedStackedInline):
    fields = ('name', 'category')
    raw_id_fields = ('category',)
    autocomplete_lookup_fields = {'fk': ('category',)}

class ParentInline(NestedStackedInline):
    fields = ('name', 'category')
    inlines = (ChildInline,)
    raw_id_fields = ('category',)
    autocomplete_lookup_fields = {'fk': ('category',)}
```

the autocomplete field 'category' in ChildInline will be initialized twice, so that two input box will appear.
in `DJNesting.initAutocompleteFields`,  `$('#' + prefix + '-group > .djn-items > *:not(.djn-empty-form)')
        .find('input[name^="' + prefix + '"][name$="-' + this + '"]')` will also find input in child inline with the same name.
currently, removing the 'category' field from autocomplete_loopup_fields in ChildInline seems ok.

issue_title: Add an option to propagate `--always-copy` to `virtualenv`
issue_body:
Some packages (`sip` from PyQt in my case), are installing some header files in the virtualenv, currently the `include/` folder is symlinked to the system _non-`nt`_ one, and so non writable.

The `virtualenv` script offers the `--always-copy` option (active by default on `nt` systems) to copy all the files.

The `pew` script by default forwards all the unknown arguments to `virtualenv`.

I have the local patch working because I'd like to use `pipenv` in my project.

NOTE: `venv` has a different argument, `--symlinks` / `--copies`, with the same use, but `pew` currently uses `virtualenv`.

- [x] add `--always-copy` option to `pipenv install`
- [ ] add `PIPENV_ALWAYS_COPY` env var
- [ ] add tests

issue_title: pipenv install doesn't install the package itself
issue_body:
There are a ton of issues mentioning `pipenv install`, so this one might already be present, or for some reason intentional, so apologies in advance if so.

##### Issue description

`pipenv install` doesn't actually install the package itself (just its dependencies).

##### Expected result

It should install the package itself as well.

##### Actual result

It doesn't.

##### Steps to replicate

Run `pipenv install`, then `pipenv run python -m pip list`, and the package isn't installed in the virtualenv.

issue_title: vendoring logic subtly breaks later usage of unvendored system versions after importing pkg_resources.extern
issue_body:
In theory, if pkg_resources.extern is devendored, it should defer to the system installation of e.g. packaging, which means that:

```
>>> packaging.version
<module 'pip._vendor.packaging.version' from '/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/packaging/version.py'>
>>> pkg_resources.extern.packaging.version
<module 'pip._vendor.packaging.version' from '/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/packaging/version.py'>
```

It would be expected that I could then compare the results, since they're the same thing.

```
>>> pkg_resources.extern.packaging.version.parse('3.0') == packaging.version.parse('2.0')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'Version' and 'Version'
```

Oops?

For comparison, this works fine when using the similarly devendored pip.

```
>>> pip._vendor.packaging.version
<module 'packaging.version' from '/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/packaging/version.py'>
>>> packaging.version
<module 'packaging.version' from '/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/packaging/version.py'>
>>> pip._vendor.packaging.version.parse('3.0') > packaging.version.parse('2.0')
True
```

Note that pkg_resources must not be devendored from pip, or else it must be deleted from vendor/\_\_init\_\_.py, since otherwise it will be automatically imported. Even importing pkg_resources causes pip to fail as well.

```
>>> import pip._vendor.packaging.version
>>> import packaging.version
>>> pip._vendor.packaging.version.parse('3.0') > packaging.version.parse('2.0')
True
```

vs.

```
>>> import pip._vendor.packaging.version
>>> import pkg_resources.extern
>>> import packaging.version
>>> pip._vendor.packaging.version.parse('3.0') > packaging.version.parse('2.0')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'Version' and 'Version'
```

https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/5429#issuecomment-391070003 suggested the problem is deleting the global module unconditionally, whenever something vendored is in use. This strikes me as incredibly dangerous.

Currently it it not possible to upload using `setup.py upload -r myownpypi` without using an username + password in `.pypirc` (there is no auth required). Blank fields for `username` and `password` causes the script to ask for password. Shouldn't it prompt for a password first when the PyPi-Server reponds with `401 Unauthorized`?

issue_title: Both python 2.7.9 and 2.7.13 are installed in the latest python:2.7 image
issue_body:
We have a container based on python:2.7 running uwsgi, which has stopped working from a clean build at some point recently, with uwsgi failing to import datetime. Haven't been able to track down exactly why, but I'm heavily suspecting it's because python 2.7.9 is installed as `/usr/bin/python` and 2.7.13 is installed as `/usr/local/bin/python` and that uwsgi is somehow using the binary from one with the libraries from the other:

```
$ docker run python:2.7 /usr/bin/python --version
Python 2.7.9

$ docker run python:2.7 /usr/local/bin/python --version
Python 2.7.13
```

we are using release 1.19.0 of this package to migrate the workflow of a custo content type; in our upgrade step we must update existing objects, change the workflow used and remove the old one:

the code looks like this (I know this looks a little bit different from the one in the documentation, but I want to know if I can use it in this way):

```python
# -*- coding:utf-8 -*-
from collective.liveblog.interfaces import ILiveblog
from collective.liveblog.logger import logger
from ftw.upgrade.workflow import WorkflowChainUpdater
from plone import api


def migrate_liveblog_workflow(context):
    """Migrate liveblog workflow."""
    review_state_mapping = {
        ('liveblog_workflow', 'simple_publication_workflow'): {
            'private': 'private',

issue_title: option for dashes at top of file
issue_body:
Our convention for YAML files is to begin with three dashes, and then to begin at a single indentation level.
```
---
  a: a
  b: b

```

When we dump an object literal like {a: 'a', b: 'b'}, the resulting file does not have the three dashes we expect. Is there an option to enable that type of behavior? Our workaround is to simply add that line, and indent every line subsequent line, after dumping the object as YAML.

Thank you for your advice!

issue_title: condenseFlow results in invalid object keys
issue_body:
```js
jsYaml.safeDump({key: 'value'}, {
    flowLevel: 0,
    indent: 0,
    lineWidth: Infinity,
    noCompatMode: true,
    sortKeys: true,
    noRefs: true,
    condenseFlow: true
})
```
results in actual YAML:
```yaml
{key:value}
```
but parsing that with `safeLoad()` results in
```js
{ 'key:value': null }
```

Expected YAML:
```yaml
{"key":value}
# or
{key: value}
```
which results in
```js
{ key: 'value' }
```

it would be best if the quoting of the key would be controlled by a `quoteKeys` setting.

cc @eseliger

issue_title: Producer in tag 2.6.0.dev2 will deadlock on a call to _update()
issue_body:
Any call to Producer._update() that finds a non-zero number of queued_messages must always deadlock.

The Producer has a non-reentrant Lock _update_lock declared here: https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/c9a5f1c7627b408f1709366c5b5ec034e0ba1db9/pykafka/producer.py#L185

The Producer's _update() function acquires the lock here: https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/c9a5f1c7627b408f1709366c5b5ec034e0ba1db9/pykafka/producer.py#L228

The Producer's _update() function calls the _produce() function while it has the _update_lock here: https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/c9a5f1c7627b408f1709366c5b5ec034e0ba1db9/pykafka/producer.py#L238

The Producer's _produce() function tries to acquire the _update_lock here: https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/c9a5f1c7627b408f1709366c5b5ec034e0ba1db9/pykafka/producer.py#L371

This sequence of events will always result in a deadlock.

I found this deadlock while looking for solutions to hung producer problems I have been having and using a RLock seemed to fix the problem.  I don't know enough about how the Producer is supposed to work to know if using an RLock is an acceptable solution.

issue_title: Rebalancing will fail after connection to zookeeper reconnects.
issue_body:
Once the connection from one consumer to zookeeper losts, the consumer_id on zookeeper will be deleted. After reconnecting to zookeeper, rebalancing of this consumer will fail. The following line at func _decide_partitions:
participants.index(self._consumer_id)
will raise an exception.
Finally, all threads calling _rebalance will exit. But the worker thread still fetch messages from broker.

I think that it should check if the consumer is still on zookeeper when _consumers_changed is called. If not, calling _add_self registers this consumer again. In the meantime, if register failed, we should exit all other threads. BTW, can you provide a function to check if the consumer is running?

issue_title: [RFC] Cache intersphinx inventory for NumPy / SciPy / Scikit-Learn
issue_body:
Sphinx 1.3 adds the ability to specify a per-inventory (set of) fallback(s) in `conf.py` if loading intersphinx inventories fail: http://sphinx-doc.org/ext/intersphinx.html

SourceForge has been having problems all day long, which has kept the scikit-learn.org page offline. Thus, all of our builds fail when they attempt to load the intersphinx inventory from scikit-learn.

The benefits are obvious, but this would mean requiring Sphinx 1.3 for building the docs and periodically updating the cache.

issue_title: Update initial values for increment and decrement
issue_body: Through integration testing, I found inc = 0.05 and dec = 0.008 to be optimal for synaptic learning.

#462 
There is no termios on windows so we need to do something better.

I documented the use of a `falsy` value, which was intended to mean "anything that evaluates to `False`". Somehow this got turned into the string value `"falsy"`, which in fact evaluates to `True` and may cause all sorts of confusion in the future. This needs to be fixed.

issue_title: add support for multi document yams files
issue_body: Please add support for multi document yaml files.  Yaml documents are separated by ```---```.

Over in conda-forge, @synapticarbors has noticed the placeholder values of the license file in this repository [and has requested](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/pull/2451#discussion_r101811429) that I open an issue here to ask that the [license file](https://github.com/saltstack/pytest-logging/blob/master/LICENSE) in this repo be completed to fill in variables, like dates and the copyright holder. I'd guess the vast majority of repositories never bother to do this, and I question whether it has legal meaning at all since the license is very clearly associated with a timestamped project of a particular user, but nonetheless I'd like to request that the license be fleshed out so I can hopefully get pytest-logging into conda-forge for distribution.

issue_title: setup: easy way to install with PostgreSQL
issue_body:
Currently, when one wants to install Invenio v3.0 with PostgreSQL, one has to explicitly install `Invenio-DB` first:

```shell
pip install invenio-db[postgresql]
pip install invenio[full] --pre
```

It would be good to enrich `setup.py`'s `extras_required` targets to allow direct DB configuration, for example:

```
pip install invenio[full,postgresql] --pre
```
It would be nice if dictdiffer had support for ordered dictionaries.

issue_title: cli: removal of `input_type` argument for `create` command
issue_body:
## Proposal

Use UNIX philosophy and combine utilities as follows:

```console
$ wget http://demo.invenio-software.org/search?of=xm -O | dojson -t dojson.contrib.marc21:marc21 | inveniomanage records create 
```

Reiteration on #29

CLI conventions:
- Max 2 levels
- Command naming:
  - <cmd> <noun> <verb>
  - no compound names (e.g. ``usercreate``)

Open questions:
- Plural vs singular?

```console
$ inveniomanages user create
$ inveniomanages role add
```
vs.
```console
$ inveniomanages users create
$ inveniomanages roles add
```

http://docs.zope.org/zope.schema/ is for zope.schema 4.2, and this is quite problematic as http://docs.zope.org/zope.schema/hacking.html#getting-the-code points to the SVN repo instead of github.

(Moved to `zope.interface` from https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.component/issues/5, because the interface now lives in `zope.interface.interfaces`).

In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.component/+bug/158580, @zopyx reported:

> `zope/component/interfaces.py` contains this for `unregisterAdaper()` which is appearently wrong (seems to be the same docstring as for `registerAdapter()`)

``` python
def unregisterAdapter(factory=None, required=None,
                          provided=None, name=u''):
        """Register an adapter factory

        A boolean is returned indicating whether the registry was
        changed. If the given component is None and there is no
        component registered, or if the given component is not
        None and is not registered, then the function returns
        False, otherwise it returns True.

        Parameters:

        factory
            This is the object used to compute the adapter. The
            factory can be None, in which case any factory
            registered to implement the given provided interface
            for the given required specifications with the given
            name is unregistered.

        required
            This is a sequence of specifications for objects to be
            adapted. If the factory is not None and the required
            arguments is omitted, then the value of the factory's
            __component_adapts__ attribute will be used. The
            __component_adapts__ attribute attribute is normally
            set in class definitions using adapts function, or for
            callables using the adapter decorator. If the factory
            is None or doesn't have a __component_adapts__ adapts
            attribute, then this argument is required.

        provided
            This is the interface provided by the adapter and
            implemented by the factory. If the factory is not
            None and implements a single interface, then this
            argument is optional and the factory-implemented
            interface will be used.

        name
            The adapter name.

        An Unregistered event is generated with an IAdapterRegistration.
```

issue_title: geocoders.base.Geocoder._call_geocoder() does not sent HTTP headers breaking Nominatim usage
issue_body:
`osm.Nominatim` expects the `User-Agent` as HTTP header otherwise it returns a HTTP-403. But there is no way to get `_call_geocoder()` to send this header:

* `requester` is not set by `Nominatim.geocode`, so `urllib.urlopen` is used as requester. But if there is a requester only the `url` is sent but not the headers. See https://github.com/geopy/geopy/blob/master/geopy/geocoders/base.py#L122 and the following lines.
* I could hardly think of a use-case where `bool(requester)` is `False` but the requester is callable, I am not sure what the initial use-case was for the code. (#L143)

Locally I now use a `class` derived from `osm.Nominatim` which converts `url` to a `Request` (as in https://github.com/geopy/geopy/blob/master/geopy/geocoders/base.py#L137) before calling `_call_geocoder()` with this request object as url. As not knowing the use-case for the code in `_call_geocoder()` I do not feel empowered to write a pull-request to get this fixed in the library.

I tried this code below:    

```
from geopy.geocoders import Nominatim
geolocator = Nominatim()
location = geolocator.geocode("San Francisco")
print location.address
```

And get result as：

> San Francisco, Agusan Del Sur, Caraga, Philippines

Obviously, it should be United States of America. What happened here? I am trying to use city name to find the country name.

issue_title: Fread should use same default column names as Frame
issue_body:
Currently fread uses "V" prefix and starts counting columns from 0; at the same time `Frame()` uses "C" prefix and starts counting from 1:
```
>>> import datatable as dt
>>> dt.Frame([[1], [2], [3]])
     C1  C2  C3
---  --  --  --
 0    1   2   3

[1 row x 3 columns]

>>> dt.fread("1,2,3\n")
     V0  V1  V2
---  --  --  --
 0    1   2   3

[1 row x 3 columns]
```

I think a reasonable compromise is to use prefix "C" (short for Column) but start counting from 0 (to be  more in sync with row count which already starts at 0)

This proposal suggests switching from the current "NFF" binary format for saving datatable Frames on disk, to a new format codenamed `Jay`. The new format saves the data into a single file (not folder) and keeps meta information in `Cap'n Proto` format (not CSV).

It is believed that the new format will be an improvement over NFF in several respects:
1. Single file is easier to pass around from one machine to another.
2. For a dataset containing multiple columns (10s or 100s of thousands), the operating system becomes very sluggish with any file-related operation.
3. Frames containing many columns but few rows waste a lot of disk space in NFF format due to each file having a minimum size of 4096 bytes.
4. A frame containing `n` columns requires between `n` and `2n` open file handles or memmapped regions, which becomes difficult on systems where those resources are limited. This becomes especially problematic in row-by-row operations on the frame.
5. The architecture that forces us to keep many memmap regions opened at the same time is prone to sporadic memory errors.
6. Cap'n Proto storage of meta information is more flexible and can be parsed faster than CSV.

The NFF format has the following advantages over Jay:
1. Multi-file storage is advantageous over single-file for gigantic datasets (100s of GB)
2. One file per column allows an administrator to control permissions for each column.
3. Having one file per column allows extending the dataset with new data more easily.

In short, NFF has several purely theoretical advantages, whereas Jay has many practical ones.

## Jay format
```
  "Jay1\0\0\0\0"       -- 8 bytes
  [data section]       -- ...
  [meta section]       -- <meta size>
  <meta size>          -- 8 bytes
  "\0\0\0\0Jay1"       -- 8 bytes
```
* The size of the file must be a multiple of 8 bytes, and all sections are aligned at 8-byte boundaries.
* The file begins and ends with `"Jay1"` string padded with 4 NUL bytes (`\0`). 
* Eight bytes before the last contain the size of the meta section (as an `int64` written in little-endian format). 
* The "meta section" is at the offset `16 + meta_size` from the end of the file, and it contains all meta information about the frame (including all column descriptors) serialized in Cap'n Proto format. 
* The portion of the file starting at offset 8 and ending at the beginning of the meta section is considered the data section. This section contains data buffers for each column. The exact location of each data buffer within the data section is stored in the meta section. The data buffers need not be adjacent to each other, and are not necessarily stored in the same order as the columns themselves. However each data buffer must be aligned at 8-byte boundary.

## Saving a frame into a Jay file
* Create a "meta info" Cap'n Proto object
* For each column in the frame, write its buffers into the file, and its meta information into the meta object (including the locations of each buffer).
* Once all columns are written, write the meta object into the file
* Write the size of the meta, and the final signature.

## Opening a Jay file
* Open the file and verify its "Jay1" signatures at the beginning/end
* Read the meta size at the end of the file.
* Read the meta object with the information about the frame and all columns.
* At this point there are 2 choices: we can either memmap the entire file, and create each Column object as a subview into the large memmapped region; or memmap each portion of the file into a separate lazy column (similar how we do with NFF).

issue_body:
It seems that python client is always opens owner connection with empty uuid's. This causes server to give new uuid to it every time owner connection is broken.

Since a new endpoint can not be registered to server for an existing connection. Old endpoint will be taking into account on server. And this result with endpoint deleted and connection closed second time. 

Instead, pyhton client should always send existing uuid's to server when authenticating.

issue_title: Use last known memberlist in reconnect
issue_body:
Current behaviour: It clears the last known memberlist in reconnect here:
https://github.com/hazelcast/hazelcast-python-client/blob/master/hazelcast/cluster.py#L220

We expect a test like following to pass normally:

```
    def test_reconnect_toNewNode_ViaLastMemberList(self):
        old_member = self.cluster.start_member()
        config = ClientConfig()
        config.network_config.addresses.append("127.0.0.1:5701")
        config.network_config.smart_routing = False
        config.network_config.connection_attempt_limit = 100
        client = self.create_client(config)
        new_member = self.cluster.start_member()
        old_member.shutdown()

        def assert_member_list():
            self.assertEqual(1, len(client.cluster.members))
            self.assertEqual(new_member.uuid, client.cluster.members[0].uuid)

        self.assertTrueEventually(assert_member_list)

```

Looks like I missed a bug in SED.py.
Here's an example:
```
import galsim
a=galsim.SED("path_to_GalSim/examples/data/CWW_E_ext.sed")
a=a.atRedshift(0.1)
a=a.thin(rel_err=1e-5)
```
gives
```ERROR:astropy:ValueError: Wavelength (128.0) is bluer than SED blue limit (140.8)```
The reason is that redshifting doesn't alter the .wave_list attribute.
This is an easy fix: two lines in SED.py:atRedshift():
```
if len(ret.wave_list) > 0:
    ret.wave_list *= wave_factor
```

Additionally, I think we should add a roundoff error guard to `SED.__init__()` similar to what's in `Bandpass.__init__()`:

```
        if len(self.wave_list) > 0.0:
            self.wave_list[0] = self.wave_list[0] + 0.0000001
            self.wave_list[-1] = self.wave_list[-1] - 0.0000001
```

This stops LookupTable from complaining when floating point roundoff leads to self.wave_list[0] > (self.wave_list[0] * factor) / factor, which is effectively how the SED class deals with redshifts and units.  It's definitely a hack though, so if anybody has a more elegant solution I'd love to hear it.

issue_title: Little/minor proposed change in the docs for the REST API endpoints (https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/docs/SETTINGS.rst)
issue_body:
Hi,
Currently, they read as:

_REST API
connect_address: IP address and port to access the REST API.
listen: IP address and port that Patroni will listen to, to provide health-check information for HAProxy._

I suggest to change them to:

_REST API
connect_address: IP address (or hostname) and port, to access the REST API.
listen: IP address (or host name) and port that Patroni will listen to, to provide health-check information for HAProxy (or any other capable load balancer capable of doing a HTTP "OPTION" or "GET" check.)_

I have this section working that way (with hostnames) without problems.  The added comment about HAProxy is because people are using other load balancers (as the http check OPTION/GET is common)

issue_title: add option to add margin of error for any measure added
issue_body:
Margins of error are an important part of any analysis. Adding the ability to easily add them is therefore an important feature for a data scientist.

We should be able to pull this off in `cc.data` by adding a `include_moe=True|False`.

Some barriers I see:
+ Is there a clear mapping between a measure and it's margin of error?
+ We'd need to do derivative calculations on MOEs for DO measures that are derived. E.g., if a measure is returned as the rate of poverty (Census Total Population / Number Poverty last 12 months), then we'd need to do the appropriate calculating to get the new error. It might be something like the square root of the sum of squares.

cc @hannahblue

After fiddling around a bit, I recalled entering an int-value in the `./chalice/config.json` file in one of my environment variables. Making this value a string instead fixed my problem.

When you create a session in sqlalchemy the `bind` parameter can be either a `Engine` or a `Connection`, but if a  `Connection` is used `parse_bind` will break.

issue_title: Weight decay causes loaded model to not match saved one
issue_body:
This has caused me a lot of frustration until I finally figured out why my saved models' results don't match when I load them.
After training a model and saving it, I expect it to produce exactly the same results as just before it was saved (assuming no updates were done in between, of course). However, this is not the case when using weight decay. Looks like the weight decay does not apply to the loaded model, even though it is set globally in `dynet_config`.

Minimal working example:
```
import dynet_config
dynet_config.set(weight_decay=1e-5)
import dynet as dy

m1 = dy.ParameterCollection()
p1 = m1.add_parameters(1)
t = dy.SimpleSGDTrainer(m1)
p1.expr().forward()
p1.expr().backward()
t.update()
dy.renew_cg()
v1 = p1.expr().value()
dy.save("test", [p1])
m2 = dy.ParameterCollection()
[p2] = dy.load("test", m2)
v2 = p2.expr().value()
assert v1 == v2, "%s != %s" % (v1, v2)
# >>> AssertionError: -1.5506035089492798 != -1.5506190061569214
```
Changing `weight_decay` to 0 fixes the problem.

Related to #917.

Module is being imported, Issue when trying to use ness6rest

![screenshot_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/7687847/24282472/a102fc22-1085-11e7-9c1b-6e9d3bbd0009.png)

The current layout of the [**Grid** class](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/generated/pyart.core.Grid.html#pyart.core.Grid) is historical and ad-hoc, it does not follow a formal standard.  A more formal layout of the class should be used which conforms to the [CF conventions](http://cfconventions.org/).  The section of the CF convention which are relavent are [5.6 Horizontal Coordinate Reference Systems, Grid Mappings, and Projections](http://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-conventions/cf-conventions-1.6/build/cf-conventions.html#grid-mappings-and-projections) and [Appendix F. Grid Mappins](http://cfconventions.org/Data/cf-conventions/cf-conventions-1.6/build/cf-conventions.html#appendix-grid-mappings).

A specification for [Gridspec](http://www.gfdl.noaa.gov/~vb/gridstd/gridstd.html#gridstdse2.html) standard has been put forward as a possible method for describing gridding data within the CF conventions.  This standard is aimed at storing model data but may be adaptable for storing gridding radar data.

There is also the [UGRID Conventions](https://github.com/ugrid-conventions/ugrid-conventions) for specifying unstructured grids.

issue_title: Bigtable: Instance object cannot create a Table with app profile
issue_labels: [{'id': 210703442, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMTA3MDM0NDI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20bigtable', 'name': 'api: bigtable', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw1NDQ2MDQyNDQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NzQzODU4Mg==', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20feature%20request', 'name': 'type: feature request', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The [`Instance` object][instance] has a method [`table()`][instance#table] which only takes a table id, but not an app profile. This means it's not possible to create a `Table` object given an instance to be used with app profiles, even though the `Table` object constructor does take an optional app profile parameter.

Thus, instead of a simple quickstart as follows:

```python
from google.cloud import bigtable

# Warning: this code doesn't work (yet)
client = bigtable.Client(project=project_id)
instance = client.instance(instance_id)
table = instance.table(table_id, app_profile_id)    # clean, simple; alas, does not work
```

we have to do something slightly round-about:

```python
from google.cloud import bigtable

client = bigtable.Client(project=project_id)
instance = client.instance(instance_id)
table = bigtable.table.Table(table_id, instance, app_profile_id)   # awkward
```

Proposal: extend `Instance#table()` to take an optional `app_profile_id` parameter that would be forwarded to the `Table` constructor and hence allow the simpler code pattern above.

Thoughts?

/cc: @sduskis, @billyjacobson, @jabubake, @ghaisa

[instance]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/a5b05d7fc50a0107a0d4f1371f6a1ea4959370f4/bigtable/google/cloud/bigtable/instance.py#L37
[instance#table]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/a5b05d7fc50a0107a0d4f1371f6a1ea4959370f4/bigtable/google/cloud/bigtable/instance.py#L256

issue_title: [Documentation] Add a nice search experience in the website
issue_body:
👋  Google team,

In our project, we need to use your awesome tool. But without search within your documentation, browsing it might be painful. In order to help me learn your tool, I have build a quick search experience that provide me a learn-as-you-type experience. I feel like I must share it to you

I'm working at Algolia on the a project called [DocSearch](https://community.algolia.com/docsearch/) which goal is to enhance documentation websites with exhaustive, fast and relevant search. You might have seen DocSearch live already on websites like [Bootstrap](https://getbootstrap.com/), [ESLint](https://eslint.org/) or [Babel](https://babeljs.io/).

We put together [a demo](https://docsearch-google-cloud-python.netlify.com/) of what DocSearch on the google-cloud-python website could look like here. Feel free to try it and let us know what you think.

[![demo of DocSearch + google-cloud-python](https://cl.ly/41f739a1ccb8/Screen%20Recording%202018-08-30%20at%2011.41%20AM.gif)](https://docsearch-google-cloud-python.netlify.com/)

The way DocSearch works is by crawling your content, pushing the results into an Algolia index, and then requesting this index directly from the website front-end through JavaScript.

We'll take care of crawling your website and populating the Algolia index with the latest changes every 24h for you. You don't need to change anything to your deployment process. The only thing you need to add are the following CSS and JS snippets that will bind the dropdown to your searchbox.

```html
<!-- at the end of the HEAD -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/docsearch.js@2/dist/cdn/docsearch.min.css" />

<!-- at the end of the BODY -->
<script type="text/javascript" src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/docsearch.js@2/dist/cdn/docsearch.min.js"></script>
<script type="text/javascript"> docsearch({
  apiKey: '783cae1144e2bc58975f4eead301c9b2',
  indexName: 'google_cloud_python',
  inputSelector: '### REPLACE ME ####',
  debug: false // Set debug to true if you want to inspect the dropdown
});
</script>
```

The `inputSelector` option should be set to a CSS selector that target your search input. Feel free to update it if this one is not specific enough.

We built DocSearch with the idea of giving back to the Open-Source community. This is why your [crawling configuration](https://github.com/algolia/docsearch-configs/blob/master/configs/google-cloud-python.json) is available on GitHub if you want to change it. We also have our own [documentation](https://community.algolia.com/docsearch/documentation/) to help you tweak the dropdown to your needs. You'll also have access to analytics on the most searched terms or those with no results. All of this is of course entirely free.

Would that be something you'd be interested in? If so, we'd be happy to work on integrating it through a PR.

issue_title: pubsub API: allow registering a callback for batches of messages
issue_body:
The old API allowed you to operate on batches of messages. Sometimes, this is advantageous as you can aggregate other calls. With the current API, this is non trivial.

So, I think (if the underlying code supports it), adding, either a parameter to open(), or another function open_many() that'll always return a list (there could be a limit in the flow control), even if it's just with one item would be useful.

We're experiencing issues where our subscription client stops pulling messages from topics that have messages. No error messages are coming through explaining what is happening. We're running the subscription client as a docker container in a kubernetes cluster. Since the subscriber runs background threads, the main process of time.sleep(30) never fails so kubernetes doesn't know to kill the pod and restart it.

https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/pull#subscriber-error-listener-go doesn't have a python code example. What do you recommend for listening to errors, and what should I use as a healthcheck for kubernetes?

issue_title: Consider tagging releases with their version instead
issue_body:
The last stable release (20170523) used a `version` string of 2.3.7. 

To be more in line with how most open source projects release stuff, I suggest that you use the version as the tag for your releases in the future. That is, the next release could be tagged `v2.4.0` instead of the release date.

After
```
pip3 download dill --no-binary :all:
```
then extracting the package, then
* build good
* upload failes, when using python3 (python2 is fine)

Reason is that in this projects's `setup.py` line 247, argument `classifiers` should be an array, not a tuple. Replacing `(` by `[` fixes the issue.

issue_title: Some helpers don't work on LegacyDatatableView
issue_body: Because default_value don't go into the kwargs. Example code: ``` def get_bool_check_mark(object, **kwargs): if kwargs.get('default_value', None): return '✔' else: return '✘' class LegacyPersonDatatableView(LegacyDatatableView): model = Person template_name = "school/persons_grid_dt.html" datatable_options = { 'columns': ['id', ('username', modal_link_to_model), 'first_name', 'last_name', 'email', 'school', ('is_active', get_bool_check_mark), ('is_pending', get_bool_check_mark), ('is_staff', get_bool_check_mark), ('is_school_contact', get_bool_check_mark), 'actions'] } structure_template = "datatableview/bootstrap_structure.html" ``` Here modal_link_to_model works but not the get_bool_check_mark.
Current implementation was a last-minute hack and there's a bunch of duplicate code and terrible names that need to be cleaned up.

issue_title: ValueError: list.remove(x): x not in list
issue_body:
Something must be broken with PEX_IGNORE_ERRORS

```
$ pex flask -o /tmp/flask.pex
$ PEX_PYTHON=pypy /tmp/flask.pex
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/flask.pex/.bootstrap/_pex/pex.py", line 311, in execute
    working_set = self._activate()
  File "/tmp/flask.pex/.bootstrap/_pex/pex.py", line 76, in _activate
    for dist in env.activate():
  File "/tmp/flask.pex/.bootstrap/_pex/environment.py", line 132, in activate
    self._working_set = self._activate()
  File "/tmp/flask.pex/.bootstrap/_pex/environment.py", line 179, in _activate
    resolved = self._resolve(working_set, all_reqs)
  File "/tmp/flask.pex/.bootstrap/_pex/environment.py", line 150, in _resolve
    reqs.remove(unresolved_req)
ValueError: list.remove(x): x not in list

```

So upgrading to pants 1.3.0 dev releases, I ran into an issue with an whl we have as a pre-release, pex is more picky with these than pip at the moment:

```
$ ls wheelhouse/pydicom-1.0.0a1-py2-none-any.whl 
wheelhouse/pydicom-1.0.0a1-py2-none-any.whl
$ pip install pydicom==1.0.0a1 -f wheelhouse/ --no-index
Collecting pydicom==1.0.0a1
Installing collected packages: pydicom
Successfully installed pydicom-1.0.0a1
$ pex pydicom==1.0.0a1 -f wheelhouse/ --no-index
Could not satisfy all requirements for pydicom==1.0.0a1:
    pydicom==1.0.0a1
```

Looking through pep 440 and pip code, it looks like if any specifier in a set is inclusive and specifies a pre-release, then pre-releases should be accepted.

It seems like that could just be a check here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/d70f2c4e679770a586c765eaaa5ce6466bbc8879/pex/package.py#L82 if operator is inclusive and version is prerelease?

[Bintray Upload] Bintray response: 200 OK. 
Done. Your build exited with 0.
/Users/travis/build.sh: line 148: shell_session_update: command not found
```

While removing Storage awareness from Scheduler, the API for ProductGraph walks and Engine root-product lookups got much more awkward.

While ProductGraph should definitely not be aware of Storage, user-facing methods of Engine should probably have facades that allow for (lazily?) executing Storage lookups during graph walks, so that users don't have to do it.

On a related note, we should probably begin to move more of the public/user-facing methods onto Engine, rather than having half of the methods an end user consumes live on Scheduler, and half on Engine.
The argument is just there to support the now-deprecated `invalidate` task.

issue_title: Better estimation for what to upload when remoting CAS misses
issue_body:
[This code](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/afaf19876e08165f7e3af66b45a8f6a0d33aa347/src/rust/engine/fs/src/store.rs#L223-L293) is called when a remote execution server tells us that it was missing some blobs in its content-addressed storage.

The `Vec<Digest>` it receives may contain any mix of digests of `Files`, and digests of `Directories`.

`Directories` are pure metadata; binary-serialized protocol buffers of lists of `Files` and `Directories` which are in a named directory.

If the remote side is missing a `Directory`, it cannot tell us which `File`s or `Directories` nested in it are also missing.

This code currently attempts to upload all transitive children of any missing `Directory`. This may be wasteful - in the extreme case, all of the `Directory`'s children are already present on the remote side, and only the top-level Directory metadata is missing.

The `remote::ByteStore` in that same file already has an address of a CAS server, which implements the [ContentAddressableStorageService gRPC API](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/afaf19876e08165f7e3af66b45a8f6a0d33aa347/3rdparty/protobuf/googleapis/google/devtools/remoteexecution/v1test/remote_execution.proto#L156-L230) which has a function to return the missing digests from a list of digests.

We should modify our `ensure_remote_has_recursive` function to use some heuristics to decide whether to all of the files, or whether to ask the server which files it needs, and just upload those. Some inputs that come to mind for this heuristic:
 * Number of files to maybe upload
 * Size of files in bytes to maybe upload
 * Other data we may have (e.g. we could keep a list of "recently uploaded files" to model what we believe the server probably has)

I recommend tacking this in stages:
1. Give the `remote::ByteStore` a `cas_client` field as well as a `bytestream_client` field (which is currently named `client`)
2. Write a new method on `remote::ByteStore` which calls the `FindMissingBlobs` API. Include some tests :)
-- Cut a PR for the above --
3. Modify `ensure_remote_has_recursive` to implement some heuristic for determining whether to filter its expanded digests list using the new `FindMissingBlobs` method.

issue_title: PythonInterpreterCache is not robust to changes in `setuptools` and/or `wheel` versions.
issue_body:
Currently the cache is just keyed by the `PythonIdentity`, eg:  `CPython-2.7.14`. If we cache an interpreter with a given resolved (setuptools-x.y.z, wheel-a.b.c), upgrade Pants with changed versions of `setuptools` and/or `wheel`, and then attempt to pull a `CPython-2.7.14` interpreter from the cache, we fail.

This was noticed in CI runs of 1.3.x which were picking up master caches from Travis CI in #5068

issue_title: OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files When calling get_data in reverse order
issue_body:
When calling `get_data` in reverse order on a video file, ffmpeg processes fail to close properly, causing an `OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files` error.

Here is some simple code that causes the error in v 2.1.1:

```
import imageio
mov = imageio.get_reader('movie.mp4')
N = mov.get_length()
for i in range(N):
    im = mov.get_data(N-1-i)
```

If the movie is long enough, this will cause the error. But there are no problems reading forwards, from frame 0 to the end.

Appears to be related to #145.

issue_body:
Currently, the user-edit modal will preemptively close, even before the response from the server is returned.

Instead, it should go into a 'busy' state while the asynchronous work is pending. On success, the modal should close. On failure, appropriate messaging should be displayed in the modal.
 
migrated from: https://trello.com/c/VOJvBYTn/718-user-edit-modal-error-handling

issue_title: Don't flip input keypad in RTL mode
issue_body: Exercise number keypad is flipped
Noticed that Scout2 is not working with SAML authentication and is there any way to make it work with SAML.

```python >>> html5lib.serializer.serialize(html5lib.parse('

 

')) '

\xa0' ``` at the moment the parsing and serialising a document causes entities to be converted into special characters, including things like #00 and there is no way to pass additional entities to xml.sax.saxutils.escape. I looked into subclassing the serialiser but the escaping happens in the middle of the serialize() method at: https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/blob/master/html5lib/serializer/htmlserializer.py#L223 perhaps the class should define an entities dict to pass through the standard html5 entities and special characters or do the escaping via a class method that can be overridden?

issue_title: aldryn-blog and aldryn-newsblog should be able to coexist in the same project
issue_body: currently there are foreign key reverse naming problems

ipwb currently explicitly requires surt in the requirements.txt file. pywb, another dependency of ipwb, also includes surt. Remove the explicit surt dependency from ipwb and reference pywb's version. Per discussion in #36 w/ @ikreymer and @ibnesayeed .

issue_title: Add "timemap" relation when specifying self in a TimeMap
issue_body:
An example TimeMap at `http://localhost:5000/timemap/link/http://memento.us` would return a response body like:

```sh
<memento.us/>; rel="original",
<http://localhost:5000/timemap/link/http://memento.us>; rel="self"; type="application/link-format",
<http://localhost:5000/20130202100000/memento.us/>; rel="first memento"; datetime="Sat, 02 Feb 2013 10:00:00 GMT",
<http://localhost:5000/20140114100000/memento.us/>; rel="memento"; datetime="Tue, 14 Jan 2014 10:00:00 GMT",
<http://localhost:5000/20140115101500/memento.us/>; rel="memento"; datetime="Wed, 15 Jan 2014 10:15:00 GMT",
<http://localhost:5000/20161231110000/memento.us/>; rel="memento"; datetime="Sat, 31 Dec 2016 11:00:00 GMT",
<http://localhost:5000/20161231110001/memento.us/>; rel="last memento"; datetime="Sat, 31 Dec 2016 11:00:01 GMT"
```

Disregarding the exclusion of the URI-T for the CDXJ version of the TimeMap (#275), the rel type for self ought to also include `timemap` as define RFC7089. This also applied to CDXJ TimeMap, which ought to use the `self timemap` vs. `timemap` relation difference to distinguish itself from the other version.
The [Reconstructive](https://github.com/oduwsdl/Reconstructive) code has been updated to use ES6 Class syntax. We should upgrade to that.

issue_title: Add the ability to host content on arbitrary URLs
issue_body: Many services such as Google Webmaster Tools require you to add specific content at a website endpoint to prove domain ownership. This typically requires updating the root URLconf and adding a template file to the project, followed by an app update. It would be much more useful if the CMS had a module, similar to the redirects module, which took an arbitrary URL and returned specified content (could just be text/plain content).

For client-side TLS connectivity to rabbitmq nodes it is important to use hostnames rather than IP addresses to validate server TLS certificates.

`dataset.add_country_locations` and `dataset.add_country_location` both allow countries to be _added_ to a dataset on HDX but don't clear out the existing set of countries (even if `dataset['groups'] = []` first).
